translation.content: As part of our countdown to RA.1000, a milestone in the 19-year history of Resident Advisor's weekly mix series, we're switching up the usual format. The next few editions will pair two acts who complement each other's strengths, offering a fresh take on a particular community, scene or style. Following on from two Nyege Nyege all-stars, this week we're zeroing in on Ron Trent and Ash Lauryn, two names who fly the flag high for old-school US house—a foundational pillar of the electronic underground. Trent takes the A-side. Hailing from Chicago, the genre's birthplace, he's a pioneer and a master of finding the sweet spot where machine music meets organic instrumentation. Listen to any DJ set or record in his extensive back catalogue and you'll find a wealth of exquisitely expressive melodies and deep, spiritual frequencies. (If you've only got a moment, sink into "Morning Factory," nine minutes of total perfection.) Trent's return to the RA Podcast, 19 years after his debut in 2006, represents his decades of expertise in fusing funk with gorgeous musicianship under the umbrella of heart-stirring house. Some may call it a masterclass but to us, it's just Trent doing what he does best. Listened to as a whole, RA.996 is a powerful snapshot of house music's elegance, class and, most importantly, understated yet innately euphoric joy. -Nyshka Chandran You're considered a pioneer of deep house but that genre no longer defines you. Your recent records, including Lift Off and What Do the Stars Say to You, feature a wide range of sounds, from jazz to ambient to boogie. What's the intention behind showcasing this expansive palette to the world? Well to be honest, my artistry has a wide range of influences not based on genre—it's more based on taste. I do know that there are different levels of things and I keep that in mind with my presentations. My musical and visual scope is layered in a time and historical context that has developed my appreciation for different styles and dialects of sound. All of these genres aforementioned have been a part of my palette one way or another. So doing an album like the WARM, or my latest, Lift Off, comes as a natural progression. In your recent interview for Rush Hour about Lift Off, you mentioned you spent a lot of time re-discovering your voice and refining it. What are some activities or practices that help you do that? Well it's a combination of things. I developed my own regiment during the time I spent developing these last two albums. A lot of it was therapeutic and highly introspective. I won't get into all of it but I will say that I listened to a lot of music, studied the mechanics of the guitar and watched as many documentaries as I could. Reference material for me means a lot. One must study to achieve higher learning. No shortcuts there. Does your time in the studio influence your DJ sets or vice-versa? Curious to hear if one impacts the other in any way. Absolutely. Whatever sound I am channeling will make its way through creatively. Working with sound the way I do it comes with a flow. Translating what is there in my studio or with the people that are in front of me is a part of a wave of energy coming through at that moment. How one affects the other is based on how I am expressing myself at the time. I've definitely made compositions that are made or inspired by a dance floor, listening or sound system. The rest of my process happens in the moment—the rest lies in mystery. Correction, July 9th: The RA Podcast started 18 years ago, in 2006, not 24.
translation.blurb: The first half of RA.996 is Ron Trent's take on luxurious house.
